seed.title: FSD unlock consequence map
seed.prompt: Given the thesis that full self-driving crosses from maybe-someday to solved-enough deployment, surface consequences and select survivors under diverge and converge.
seed.thesis: Vision-based autonomy becoming legally and operationally real removes several substrates: crash risk, human driver time, traffic enforcement subjecthood, and private car ownership.
goals: prove one shared generated candidate pool can support both dials
keep novelty and grounding visible until selection
show which generated children survive, stabilize, or get replaced by the other dial

operators.label: Substrate removal
operators.description: Find the human, institutional, or economic substrate that disappears if the seed is true.

operators.label: Hidden dependent
operators.description: Follow the removed substrate into second-order actors whose business depends on it.

operators.label: Adoption boundary
operators.description: Look for uneven deployment edges where one region, actor, or belief updates before another.

operators.label: Infrastructure inversion
operators.description: Ask whether the new system becomes infrastructure for a different market.

operators.label: Cost-curve inversion
operators.description: Look for deployed assets that become liabilities when the cost curve flips.

sourcePackets.title: Crash volume dependency
sourcePackets.mechanism: If autonomy removes a large share of crash volume, industries that monetize accidents lose their event stream.

candidate.title: Accident economy dries up
candidate.thesis: The visible crash reduction branch is not just safer roads; it removes revenue and labor from insurance, plaintiff law, collision repair, trauma workflows, and organ supply.

delta.summary: Moved from safer roads to businesses dependent on accident volume.
changes: driver risk -> insurance and repair substrate

claims: Insurance and repair pools shrink when the crash event falls away.
Hidden dependents such as ad spend and trauma-side supply chains are downstream of accident volume.
evidence: large auto insurance and collision repair markets

sourcePackets.title: Human driver as enforcement subject
sourcePackets.mechanism: Traffic enforcement assumes a human driver who can be stopped, cited, searched, tested, and assigned points.

candidate.title: Traffic-state loses its subject
candidate.thesis: When the human stops being the driver, the traffic stop, ticket, pretext-search, points, DUI docket, and forfeiture pipeline lose their ordinary on-ramp.

delta.summary: Moved from autonomous driving to disappearance of the policeable driver.

claims: A passenger cannot be cited as the driver by construction.
Fine revenue and pretext-stop authority face a dry-riverbed problem.
evidence: traffic stop as common police-citizen contact

sourcePackets.title: Idle private car
sourcePackets.mechanism: Autonomy changes the private car from idle consumer asset into a potential earning fleet node.

candidate.title: Ownership becomes fleet yield
candidate.thesis: Autonomy attacks the privately owned idle car: finance, dealers, residual values, aftermarket repair, and parking all reprice around service fleets or owner-operated robotaxi yield.

delta.summary: Moved from better cars to idle owned assets repricing as yield assets.

claims: Idle time becomes monetizable only if the car can work without the owner.
The fork is pure service versus owned-autonomous fleet hybrid.
evidence: auto debt and franchise dealer structures

sourcePackets.title: Active driving time
sourcePackets.mechanism: Door-to-door autonomy converts driving time into sleep or work time, changing the cost of distance.

candidate.title: Sleeper commute rewires geography
candidate.thesis: Door-to-door autonomy changes the 200-700 mile band: sleeping or working through travel collapses the felt cost of distance and welds labor markets into megaregions.

delta.summary: Moved from urban robotaxi to distance friction disappearing in the intercity band.
changes: car trip -> geography and labor-market substrate

claims: The strongest travel substitution is not urban robotaxi; it is intercity time conversion.
Real estate and labor pools reprice when commute radius stops meaning active driving.
evidence: door-to-door travel versus aviation weak bands

sourcePackets.title: Uneven deployment edge
sourcePackets.mechanism: FSD adoption arrives in regions, regulatory contexts, and belief communities at different speeds.

candidate.title: Adoption asymmetry creates border arbitrage
candidate.thesis: FSD arrives unevenly, so the edge lives at the boundary: believers versus disbelievers, served metros versus laggards, and leapfrog regions that skip old car ownership patterns.

delta.summary: Moved from average arrival to edges where belief and deployment diverge.

claims: Average-arrival stories miss where the future is already real.
The boundary between served and unserved regions creates arbitrage.

sourcePackets.title: Autonomous fleet as edge substrate
sourcePackets.mechanism: Networked autonomous vehicles contain sensors, power, positioning, and compute that could be redeployed beyond transport.

candidate.title: Fleet compute becomes mobile edge
candidate.thesis: A large autonomous fleet could become a distributed sensing and compute substrate, turning parked or low-utilization vehicles into mobile edge infrastructure.

delta.summary: Moved from cars as transport to fleet as distributed infrastructure.

claims: Vehicles already carry sensors, power, and networked compute.
Idle fleet time might be a latent asset beyond transport.
evidence: speculative boundary noted in FSD cluster

sourcePackets.title: Sensor-heavy fleet inventory
sourcePackets.mechanism: A deployed LiDAR-heavy fleet can look like lead until camera-only OTA economics reprice the asset base.

candidate.title: LiDAR retrofit inventory is not a lead
candidate.thesis: Competitors with deployed LiDAR-heavy fleets may own expensive inventory rather than durable advantage if camera-only OTA economics dominate the cost curve.

delta.summary: Moved from deployment count to cost-curve liability.

claims: Deployment count can mislead if the cost structure is wrong.
The curve matters more than static fleet snapshots.

sourcePackets.title: Dying road-use revenue
sourcePackets.mechanism: Fine, fuel-tax, registration, and enforcement revenue lines adapt when autonomy changes driving behavior.

candidate.title: Movement metering replaces fine revenue
candidate.thesis: The state will claw back dying fine, fuel-tax, and registration revenue through VMT, congestion pricing, and fleet-level movement metering.

delta.summary: Moved from fines disappearing to states replacing the measurement basis for transport revenue.

claims: Governments do not let transport revenue lines die quietly.
Autonomous fleets make road-use pricing technically easier.

sourcePackets.title: Safer autonomous cars
sourcePackets.mechanism: Repeats the seed as a generic safety improvement without naming a new removed substrate.
sourcePackets.noDeltaReason: No new substrate or mechanism beyond the seed thesis.

sourcePackets.title: FSD becomes popular
sourcePackets.mechanism: Restates adoption as adoption, without a hidden dependent or changed causal surface.
sourcePackets.noDeltaReason: No delta: the packet names uptake but not a new dependent, substrate, or mechanism.
